title: HardInterventionNodeSplitOrder

lean_statement.name: swig_precondition_fails_on_intersection
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_12 (Scenario A): when `w ‚ąą W‚āĀ ‚ą© W‚āā` (and the LN
states this happens in `G.V`), the SWIG precondition
`W‚āā ‚äÜ (G.hardInterventionOn W‚āĀ).V` fails. Hence the LHS of
claim_3_11's identity `(G_{do(W‚āĀ)})_{swig(W‚āā)}` cannot even
be formed in our Lean framework -- so the "HI then SWIG" order
is *strictly worse* than "SWIG then HI" when `W‚āĀ ‚ą© W‚āā` meets
`G.V`. -/
theorem swig_precondition_fails_on_intersection
    {G : CDMG őĪ} {w : őĪ} {W‚āĀ W‚āā : Set őĪ}
    (hw : w ‚ąą W‚āĀ ‚ą© W‚āā) (hwV : w ‚ąą G.V) :
    ¬¨ (W‚āā ‚äÜ (G.hardInterventionOn W‚āĀ).V) := by

lean_statement.name: swig_hardInterventionOn_inputs_eq_self
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_12 (Scenario B1): for any subset `I` of the
input-copy indices, hard-intervening on `Sum.inr '' I` is a
*no-op* on `G.swig W hW`. Formalises the LN's "A hard
intervention on `w^i` would not do anything" -- the `Sum.inr`
half of the SWIG carrier is the `W^i` set, which is already
in `J` of the SWIG (so the HI's J-promotion adds nothing) and
not in `V`, with no edges incident to it (so the HI's edge
deletions are vacuous). -/
theorem swig_hardInterventionOn_inputs_eq_self
    {G : CDMG őĪ} {W : Set őĪ} (hW : W ‚äÜ G.V) (I : Set ‚ÜĎW) :
    (G.swig W hW).hardInterventionOn (Sum.inr '' I) = G.swig W hW := by

lean_statement.name: swig_hardInterventionOn_outputs_V
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_12 (Scenario B2): hard-intervening on
`Sum.inl '' S` removes exactly `Sum.inl '' S` from the SWIG's
`V`. Formalises the LN's "hard intervening on `w^o` would
turn `w^o` into an additional input node" -- the
`Sum.inl`-half is the canonical observation half, and HI
genuinely promotes those vertices out of `V`. -/
theorem swig_hardInterventionOn_outputs_V
    {G : CDMG őĪ} {W : Set őĪ} (hW : W ‚äÜ G.V) (S : Set őĪ) (hS : S ‚äÜ W) :
    ((G.swig W hW).hardInterventionOn (Sum.inl '' S)).V
      = Sum.inl '' (G.V \ S) := by

lean_statement.name: swig_then_hardInterventionOn_depends_on_copy_choice
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_12 (Punchline corollary): for `w ‚ąą G.V`, hard
intervention on `{Sum.inl w}` (the output copy, "`w^o`") gives
a *different* graph from hard intervention on
`{Sum.inr ‚ü®w, _‚ü©}` (the input copy, "`w^i`"). This formalises
the Remark's punchline that, under our `Sum.inl = w^o`,
`Sum.inr = w^i` convention, the LN's "ambiguity on which of
those two the hard intervention should be applied" yields
genuinely non-equal graphs -- so "SWIG then HI on `w`" is
*not* a well-defined operation when `w ‚ąą W`; one must commit
to a copy. -/
theorem swig_then_hardInterventionOn_depends_on_copy_choice
    {G : CDMG őĪ} {w : őĪ} (hwV : w ‚ąą G.V) :
    (G.swig ({w} : Set őĪ) (Set.singleton_subset_iff.mpr hwV)).hardInterventionOn
        ({Sum.inl w} : Set (őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎ({w} : Set őĪ)))
      ‚Č† (G.swig ({w} : Set őĪ) (Set.singleton_subset_iff.mpr hwV)).hardInterventionOn
        ({Sum.inr ‚ü®w, rfl‚ü©} : Set (őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎ({w} : Set őĪ))) := by

lean_explanation:
The LN's Remark is a narrative about why the disjointness premise of claim_3_11 is load-bearing -- when $W_1 \cap W_2 \subseteq V$ is non-empty, the order of $\doit(W_1)$ and $\swig(W_2)$ matters. The formalisation captures this through four observations corresponding to the LN's three operational scenarios (A, B1, B2) plus a punchline corollary.

Across all four sub-statements, `őĪ : Type*` is the ambient vertex type, matching the LN's silent assumption that $G$ lives over some unspecified carrier; `G : CDMG őĪ` is the LN's $G = (J, V, E, L)$. In Scenario A, `w : őĪ`, `W‚āĀ W‚āā : Set őĪ` mirror the LN's $w \in W_1 \cap W_2$, with `hw : w ‚ąą W‚āĀ ‚ą© W‚āā` and `hwV : w ‚ąą G.V` the membership facts. In Scenarios B1 / B2 and the punchline, `W : Set őĪ` (and the singleton `{w}` in the punchline) is the SWIG'd set, with `hW : W ‚äÜ G.V` discharging the SWIG precondition. `I : Set ‚ÜĎW` (B1) and `S : Set őĪ` with `hS : S ‚äÜ W` (B2) are the intervention targets after the SWIG.

Mathlib idioms worth flagging: `Sum.inl`, `Sum.inr` are the canonical injections into the post-SWIG carrier `őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎW`, with the LN's convention `Sum.inl = w^o` (the canonical observation copy) and `Sum.inr = w^i` (the new input copy); `‚ÜĎW` is the coerced subtype `{x : őĪ // x ‚ąą W}`, Lean's way of carrying the LN's input copies; `Set.image` (written infix as `''`) lifts a function pointwise to sets, so `Sum.inr '' I` is a slice of $W^i$ and `Sum.inl '' S` is the LN's $S^o$; `Set.range Sum.inr` is the whole $W^i$ half of the carrier; `Set.singleton_subset_iff.mpr hwV` packages `w ‚ąą G.V` into `{w} ‚äÜ G.V` to discharge the SWIG precondition in the punchline; `Set.image_diff Sum.inl_injective` consolidates `Sum.inl '' G.V \ Sum.inl '' S` to `Sum.inl '' (G.V \ S)` in Scenario B2.

The four theorems have these shapes. Scenario A concludes a *negation* `¬¨ (W‚āā ‚äÜ (G.hardInterventionOn W‚āĀ).V)` -- the literal SWIG precondition after the outer HI, recording that the LHS of claim_3_11's identity fails to elaborate when `W‚āĀ ‚ą© W‚āā` meets `G.V`. Scenario B1 concludes a literal `Eq` of CDMGs: hard-intervening on `Sum.inr '' I` after a SWIG returns the same SWIG, witnessed componentwise via the four `@[simp]` SWIG projections plus the four outer-HI projections. Scenario B2 concludes only the `V`-projection `Sum.inl '' (G.V \ S)`, the cleanest witness of how HI on output copies shrinks `V`. The punchline concludes `‚Č†` of two CDMGs that share the same underlying SWIG and outer operation but differ only in whether the intervention target is `{Sum.inl w}` (output copy, witnessed by B2) or `{Sum.inr ‚ü®w, rfl‚ü©}` (input copy, witnessed by B1); the disagreement element `Sum.inl w` lies in one `V` but not the other.
design_choices:
**Four observations, not a single `Eq` or `Ne`.** The LN block is a `Rem` -- a narrative discussion of non-commutativity, not a clean equality. A single `Eq` of CDMGs would force a choice of *which* of the LN's three scenarios to formalise and silently drop the other two; decomposing into four observations (Scenario A + Scenarios B1, B2 + Punchline corollary) is the only honest translation of a narrative `Rem`. The four together reconstruct the LN's argument that the disjointness premise of claim_3_11 is load-bearing, not a convenience.

**Scenario A is recorded as a precondition-failure statement.** When `w ‚ąą W‚āĀ ‚ą© W‚āā` and `w ‚ąą G.V`, hard intervention on `W‚āĀ` first removes `w` from `V`, so `W‚āā` no longer satisfies the SWIG precondition `W‚āā ‚äÜ V_{\doit(W_1)}`. The LHS of claim_3_11's identity is then ill-typed in our Lean framework. Since Lean cannot express "no proof of an ill-formed term exists" directly, the clean formal record is the negation `¬¨ (W‚āā ‚äÜ (G.hardInterventionOn W‚āĀ).V)` -- which a downstream reader can dispatch via the `@[simp]` projection `hardInterventionOn_V` to reduce the RHS to `G.V \ W‚āĀ` and a one-line set argument. The LN's parenthetical "a node-splitting hard intervention (if we would define it for input nodes) would not change $w^i$" describes a hypothetical extension that our `swig` (precondition `W ‚äÜ G.V`) does not implement; the formal content of Scenario A is therefore just the typing obstruction.

**Scenario B1 generalises from the LN's singleton to arbitrary `Sum.inr '' I`.** The LN narrates the singleton case (a single $w$), but the no-op property holds uniformly for any subset of the input-copy half: none of the SWIG's data fields are sensitive to which `Sum.inr` labels the HI promotes -- every such label is already in `J`, not in `V`, and not an endpoint of any edge. The general statement identifies the *reason* the no-op holds (a global property of `Sum.inr`'s position in the SWIG carrier) and sidesteps the awkward subtype-construction `‚ü®w, hw‚ü© : ‚ÜĎW` that the singleton form would force at every call site. The conclusion is a literal `Eq` of CDMGs because both sides have carrier `őĪ ‚äē ‚ÜĎW` and the four data fields agree on the nose; the discharge uses the local `mk_eq_of_data` helper to reduce to four constructor-mismatch checks (`Sum.inl _ ‚ąČ Sum.inr '' I` and its symmetric partner).

**Scenario B2 records only the `V`-projection, not a full CDMG equality.** Stating the full equality (with the new `J`, `E`, `L` after the intervention) would add four times the complexity for zero downstream payoff -- the purpose here is just to *exhibit* one component-level disagreement to underpin the punchline. The `V`-projection is the cleanest witness: it shrinks by exactly `Sum.inl '' S`, no more, no less, with no constructor case-split. The closed form `Sum.inl '' (G.V \ S)` rather than `(G.swig W hW).V \ Sum.inl '' S` aligns with the LN's mental model "vertex set minus the intervened set" under the identification $\alpha \cong \mathrm{inl}(\alpha)$, and consolidates the two `Sum.inl ''` images via `Set.image_diff Sum.inl_injective`.

**Punchline uses singleton `W = {w}` to match the LN narrative one-to-one.** The LN's Remark walks through a single $w \in W_1 \cap W_2$, asking "what would happen if we picked $w^i$" vs. "what would happen if we picked $w^o$". The singleton form is therefore the most LN-faithful and serves as a concrete refutation of any naive "post-SWIG HI on $w$" claim. The two CDMGs being compared share the same underlying SWIG (`G.swig {w} _`) and outer operation (`hardInterventionOn`); only the *target set* differs: `{Sum.inl w}` for the output-copy branch (reduced via Scenario B2) versus `{Sum.inr ‚ü®w, rfl‚ü©}` for the input-copy branch (reduced via Scenario B1). The disagreement is maximally crisp -- `Sum.inl w` lies in one `V` but not the other -- and the proof just chains the two sibling theorems with a single membership argument.

**Local `mk_eq_of_data` helper rather than an import.** Scenario B1's full-CDMG equality is discharged via a private ten-line componentwise-extensionality helper, declared verbatim from the sibling claim_3_11's file. `CDMG` is intentionally not `@[ext]`-tagged, so each row that needs componentwise extensionality re-declares this `private` helper -- the duplication is judged cheaper than wiring a build-graph dependency on the sibling, and the helper is not part of the public LN-facing API.

**`hwV` (Scenario A) and `hS` (Scenario B2) are LN-faithful but operationally unused.** Scenario A's conclusion follows from `w ‚ąą W‚āā` and `w ‚ąą W‚āĀ` alone; the LN's $w \in V$ qualifier places the scenario in the regime where the original $\swig(W_2)$ would have been applicable, so it documents the LN context. Scenario B2's conclusion holds for arbitrary `S : Set őĪ`, not just `S ‚äÜ W`; we keep `hS` because the LN's scenario is exactly the case `S ‚äÜ W` (the output copies of vertices that were actually split). Both hypotheses are kept rather than dropped to mirror the LN's $w \in W_1 \cap W_2 \subseteq V$ qualifier exactly and to keep downstream readers aware of the LN-intended scope.
